name: Business Technology & Digital Literacy
description: Foundational digital literacy for the modern business world — productivity tools, spreadsheets, data basics, internet skills, cybersecurity awareness, and professional digital communication.

modules.title: Digital Literacy Foundations
modules.objectives: Identify hardware and software components; understand operating systems; manage files and folders; navigate the internet safely.
modules.readingNote: Google Digital Garage — Digital Skills for Work: Module 1 (The Online World)
modules.videoNote: GCFGlobal — Computer Basics: What is a Computer?
modules.video2Note: GCFGlobal — Computer Basics: Understanding Operating Systems
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Computer Science Fundamentals: Intro activities
modules.assignment: Label a diagram of a desktop computer identifying at least 8 hardware components. Create a folder structure for a fictional school project using at least 3 levels of organization.

modules.title: Professional Email & Communication
modules.objectives: Write professional emails using correct tone, structure, and etiquette; use CC, BCC, and Reply-All appropriately; understand professional messaging norms.
modules.readingNote: Google Digital Garage — Digital Skills for Work: Email & Communication module
modules.videoNote: GCFGlobal — Email Basics: What is Email?
modules.video2Note: GCFGlobal — Email Tips: How to Write a Professional Email
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — The Internet: How email works
modules.assignment: Write 3 professional emails: (1) requesting information from a company, (2) following up after a job interview, (3) apologizing for a missed deadline. Annotate each email identifying subject line, greeting, body, and closing.

modules.title: Word Processing & Document Design
modules.objectives: Use Google Docs or Microsoft Word to create, format, and style documents; apply headings, margins, spacing, and templates; produce a professional report.
modules.readingNote: Google Digital Garage — Digital Skills for Work: Creating Documents
modules.videoNote: GCFGlobal — Google Docs: Getting Started with Google Docs
modules.video2Note: GCFGlobal — Word 2016: Formatting Text and Adding Hyperlinks
modules.practiceNote: Google Docs — Create a document using the provided template and practice formatting styles
modules.assignment: Create a 2-page report on a technology topic of your choice. Apply: title, at least 3 heading levels, body text, bullet lists, a table, and page numbers. Use a consistent font and style throughout.

modules.title: Spreadsheets: Basics
modules.objectives: Enter and format data in Google Sheets or Excel; use basic formulas: SUM, AVERAGE, IF, COUNT; understand cell references.
modules.readingNote: Google Digital Garage — Digital Skills for Work: Spreadsheet Basics
modules.videoNote: GCFGlobal — Excel 2016: Getting Started with Excel
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Intro to programming: practice data entry and logic exercises
modules.assignment: Build a personal budget spreadsheet for one month. Include income, at least 8 expense categories, and use SUM, AVERAGE, and IF formulas. Format with currency, bold headers, and conditional highlighting for over-budget cells.

modules.title: Spreadsheets: Data Analysis
modules.objectives: Sort and filter data; use VLOOKUP; create pivot tables; build charts to visualize data trends.
modules.readingNote: Google Digital Garage — Digital Skills for Work: Using Data in Spreadsheets
modules.video2Note: GCFGlobal — Excel: How to Use VLOOKUP
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Statistics & Probability: Reading and interpreting data displays
modules.assignment: Given a provided dataset of 50 sales records, (1) sort by revenue descending, (2) filter to show only records from Q3, (3) create a VLOOKUP to retrieve product names by ID, (4) build a pivot table summarizing revenue by category, (5) create a bar chart.

modules.title: Presentations & Slide Design
modules.titleZh: 演示文稿与幻灯片设计
modules.objectives: Build effective presentations in Google Slides or PowerPoint; apply design principles: visual hierarchy, color, font, and white space; structure a narrative with slides.
modules.readingNote: Google Digital Garage — Digital Skills for Work: Creating Presentations
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — What makes a truly great presentation?
modules.practiceNote: Google Slides — Design a 5-slide pitch deck using a custom theme
modules.assignment: Create a 10-slide presentation on a business idea of your choice. Apply: title slide, agenda, 6 content slides, and a closing slide. Use consistent fonts, a color palette of 3 colors, and include at least 2 data visualizations.

modules.title: Cloud Computing & Collaboration
modules.objectives: Use Google Drive or OneDrive to store, share, and collaborate on files; understand version history; apply real-time co-editing best practices.
modules.readingNote: Google Digital Garage — Digital Skills for Work: Working in the Cloud
modules.practiceNote: Google Drive — Organize a shared folder, set permissions, and use version history
modules.assignment: Set up a shared Google Drive folder simulating a team project. Create a shared Doc, Sheet, and Slides file. Practice using comments, suggestions mode, and version history. Write a one-page reflection on the benefits and challenges of cloud collaboration.

modules.title: Internet Research & Information Literacy
modules.objectives: Evaluate sources for credibility and bias; use Boolean search operators; fact-check claims; identify misinformation.
modules.readingNote: Google Digital Garage — Digital Skills for Work: Finding Things Online
modules.videoNote: TED-Ed — How to choose your news
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — How to spot a misleading graph
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Research skills: evaluating sources practice
modules.assignment: Select 3 online articles on the same news story from different sources. Evaluate each using the SIFT method (Stop, Investigate the source, Find better coverage, Trace claims). Write a 1-page comparison explaining which sources you trust most and why.

modules.title: Cybersecurity & Digital Safety
modules.objectives: Create strong passwords; recognize phishing attempts; understand malware types; configure privacy settings; practice safe browsing habits.
modules.readingNote: Google Digital Garage — Digital Skills for Work: Staying Safe Online
modules.videoNote: TED-Ed — How to create a better password
modules.video2Note: GCFGlobal — Internet Safety: Phishing Scams and How to Avoid Them
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Online Data Security: Introduction to cybersecurity
modules.assignment: Audit your personal digital security: evaluate password strength for 5 accounts, review privacy settings on 2 social media platforms, and identify 3 potential phishing red flags in a set of sample emails provided by the instructor. Write a personal cybersecurity action plan.

modules.title: Social Media & Professional Branding
modules.objectives: Understand digital footprint; create a professional LinkedIn profile; distinguish personal vs professional social media use; manage online reputation.
modules.readingNote: Google Digital Garage — Digital Skills for Work: Your Online Presence
modules.videoNote: GCFGlobal — LinkedIn Basics: Creating a Profile
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — The future of your digital self
modules.practiceNote: LinkedIn — Set up or update your student profile with education, skills, and a summary
modules.assignment: Create or update a LinkedIn profile with: professional photo description, compelling headline, 3-sentence summary, education section, and 5 relevant skills. Write a 1-page reflection on the difference between your professional and personal digital presence.

modules.title: Introduction to Data & Analytics
modules.objectives: Define data and data types; calculate and interpret descriptive statistics; read dashboards and data visualizations; understand the data analysis process.
modules.readingNote: Google Digital Garage — Digital Skills for Work: Making Sense of Data
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Statistics: Summarizing Quantitative Data
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Mean, Median, and Mode practice
modules.assignment: Collect data on 10 of your classmates' study hours per week. Calculate mean, median, mode, and range. Create a histogram and a summary paragraph interpreting your findings. Identify one insight and one question your data raises.

modules.title: Digital Project Management
modules.objectives: Use project management tools (Trello, Asana, or Notion) to plan tasks; create timelines; assign responsibilities; manage team workflows and deadlines.
modules.readingNote: Google Digital Garage — Digital Skills for Work: Project Management Basics
modules.videoNote: GCFGlobal — Trello: Getting Started with Trello
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — How to manage a project
modules.practiceNote: Trello — Create a board for a class project with To Do, In Progress, and Done columns
modules.assignment: Plan a fictional 4-week school event using Trello or Notion. Create at least 15 tasks across 3 phases, assign due dates, add labels by priority, and write a brief status update as if reporting to a manager after Week 2.

modules.title: E-Commerce & Digital Business Models
modules.objectives: Explain how online businesses operate; compare marketplace, subscription, and direct-to-consumer models; understand digital payments and customer experience.
modules.readingNote: Google Digital Garage — Digital Skills for Work: Doing Business Online
modules.videoNote: TED-Ed — How does the stock market work? (digital economy context)
modules.video2Note: GCFGlobal — E-Commerce Basics: How Online Shopping Works
modules.practiceNote: Google Digital Garage — Lesson: Sell products and services online
modules.assignment: Research and compare 2 e-commerce companies (one marketplace like Amazon, one direct-to-consumer like Warby Parker). Analyze their business model, revenue streams, and customer experience. Present your comparison in a 2-page report or a 6-slide deck.

modules.title: Emerging Technologies & the Future of Work
modules.objectives: Understand AI and automation basics; identify gig economy trends; evaluate which digital skills matter most for career readiness; reflect on technology's impact on society.
modules.readingNote: Google Digital Garage — Digital Skills for Work: Future of Work & Technology
modules.videoNote: TED-Ed — How AI could become an extension of your mind
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — The jobs we'll lose to machines — and the ones we won't
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Algorithms: Intro to algorithms (foundations for understanding AI)
modules.assignment: Write a 1-page reflection: (1) identify 2 jobs that AI/automation may significantly change within 10 years, (2) name 3 digital skills you already have and 3 you want to develop, and (3) describe one technology trend that excites or concerns you and why.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of hardware?
options: Microsoft Word
The Windows operating system
A keyboard
A PDF file
questions.answer: A keyboard
questions.explanation: Hardware refers to the physical components of a computer. A keyboard is a physical input device.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct structure for a professional email?
options: Subject, body, emoji, signature
Subject line, greeting, body, professional closing, signature
Greeting, subject line, body, signature
Body, subject, CC, signature
questions.answer: Subject line, greeting, body, professional closing, signature
questions.explanation: Professional emails follow the structure: clear subject line, polite greeting, organized body, professional closing (e.g., 'Best regards'), and a signature.

questions.question: In an email, BCC stands for:
options: Bold Carbon Copy
Blind Carbon Copy
Basic Copy & Confirmation
Bulk Contact Channel
questions.answer: Blind Carbon Copy
questions.explanation: BCC (Blind Carbon Copy) sends a copy of the email to additional recipients without other recipients seeing their addresses.

questions.question: Which Google Docs feature allows you to see and restore a previous version of a document?
options: Spell Check
Version History
Explore Tool
Document Outline
questions.answer: Version History
questions.explanation: Version History (File > Version History) lets you view and restore earlier versions of a Google Docs document.

questions.question: In a spreadsheet, which formula would calculate the total of cells A1 through A10?
options: =TOTAL(A1:A10)
=ADD(A1,A10)
=SUM(A1:A10)
=COUNT(A1:A10)
questions.answer: =SUM(A1:A10)
questions.explanation: =SUM(A1:A10) adds all numeric values in the range A1 through A10.

questions.question: What does the AVERAGE function do in a spreadsheet?
options: Finds the largest value in a range
Counts the number of cells with data
Calculates the arithmetic mean of a range of numbers
Rounds all values to the nearest integer
questions.answer: Calculates the arithmetic mean of a range of numbers
questions.explanation: =AVERAGE(range) sums all values in the range and divides by the count of those values to return the arithmetic mean.

questions.question: Which design principle means that the most important element on a slide should visually stand out the most?
options: Color harmony
White space
Visual hierarchy
Font pairing
questions.answer: Visual hierarchy
questions.explanation: Visual hierarchy guides the viewer's eye by making the most important information larger, bolder, or higher contrast than supporting details.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes cloud storage?
options: Files stored on a USB drive
Files stored on your computer's hard drive only
Files stored on remote servers accessible via the internet
Files printed and stored physically
questions.answer: Files stored on remote servers accessible via the internet
questions.explanation: Cloud storage saves data on remote servers managed by a provider (e.g., Google Drive, OneDrive), allowing access from any internet-connected device.

questions.question: Using the Boolean search operator AND in a search engine:
options: Finds pages with either of the terms
Excludes a term from search results
Requires both terms to appear in results
Finds exact phrases only
questions.answer: Requires both terms to appear in results
questions.explanation: Boolean AND narrows a search by requiring all connected terms to be present in every result returned.

questions.question: Which characteristic is a red flag that an email might be a phishing attempt?
options: It comes from a known colleague
It asks you to verify your password by clicking a link
It has a formal greeting and signature
It was sent during business hours
questions.answer: It asks you to verify your password by clicking a link
questions.explanation: Legitimate organizations never ask for passwords via email. Urgent requests to click links and enter credentials are classic phishing tactics.

questions.question: Which of the following is the strongest password?
options: password123
JohnSmith1990
Tr#9kL!mQ2@v
questions.answer: Tr#9kL!mQ2@v
questions.explanation: Strong passwords are long, use a mix of uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols, and avoid common words or personal information.

questions.question: What is a digital footprint?
options: The physical size of a computer monitor
The amount of data your device uses per month
The trail of data you leave behind from your online activities
The resolution of images posted online
questions.answer: The trail of data you leave behind from your online activities
questions.explanation: A digital footprint is the record of everything you do online — posts, searches, purchases, and interactions — which can be traced back to you.

questions.question: When sharing a Google Drive file, setting access to 'Viewer' means the recipient can:
options: Edit and delete the file
Comment only
Only view the file without making changes
Share the file with others
questions.answer: Only view the file without making changes
questions.explanation: Viewer access allows recipients to see the file contents but not edit, comment, or share it.

questions.question: You receive an email from 'support@paypa1.com' saying your account has been suspended and you must click a link immediately to restore access. Identify two specific red flags in this scenario and explain what you should do.
questions.answer: Red flags: (1) misspelled domain name (paypa1.com uses the number '1' instead of the letter 'l'); (2) urgency/pressure tactic ('immediately'). Action: do not click the link; go directly to the official website by typing the URL; report the email as phishing.
questions.explanation: Phishing emails commonly use lookalike domains and create urgency to trick users into acting before thinking critically.

questions.question: A classmate says, 'Cloud storage is risky — I only save files on my computer.' Provide two advantages of cloud storage over local storage only, and one valid concern your classmate might have.
questions.answer: Advantages: (1) files are accessible from any internet-connected device; (2) automatic backup prevents data loss if the device is lost or damaged. Valid concern: if the internet is unavailable or the cloud provider has an outage, files may be temporarily inaccessible; also, privacy concerns about data stored on third-party servers.
questions.explanation: Cloud storage offers significant convenience and redundancy, but relying solely on the cloud introduces internet dependency and third-party privacy risks.

questions.question: Which component is the 'brain' of a computer that processes instructions?
options: RAM
Hard Drive
CPU
Monitor
questions.answer: CPU
questions.explanation: The CPU (Central Processing Unit) is the primary component that executes instructions and processes data for all computer operations.

questions.question: The subject line of a professional email should be:
options: Left blank to seem mysterious
Long and detailed to explain everything
Concise and descriptive of the email's purpose
Written in all capital letters to grab attention
questions.answer: Concise and descriptive of the email's purpose
questions.explanation: A good subject line is brief yet informative, giving the recipient a clear idea of the email's content before opening it.

questions.question: In Microsoft Word or Google Docs, 'Styles' are used to:
options: Change the document's background color
Apply consistent formatting to headings and body text throughout a document
Spell-check the entire document
Convert the document to a PDF
questions.answer: Apply consistent formatting to headings and body text throughout a document
questions.explanation: Styles (Heading 1, Heading 2, Normal, etc.) enforce consistent formatting and allow automatic generation of a table of contents.

questions.question: In a spreadsheet, which formula returns 'Pass' if the value in A1 is 60 or above, and 'Fail' otherwise?
options: =IF(A1>=60, 'Pass', 'Fail')
=IF(A1>60, 'Fail', 'Pass')
=SUM(A1, 'Pass')
=AVERAGE(A1, 60)
questions.answer: =IF(A1>=60, 'Pass', 'Fail')
questions.explanation: The IF function evaluates a condition (A1>=60) and returns the first value ('Pass') if true, or the second ('Fail') if false.

questions.question: VLOOKUP in a spreadsheet is primarily used to:
options: Look up a value in a column and return a related value from the same row
Calculate the vertical average of a column
Find the highest value in a data range
Sort data in ascending order
questions.answer: Look up a value in a column and return a related value from the same row
questions.explanation: VLOOKUP searches the first column of a range for a match and returns a value from a specified column in the same row.

questions.question: Which chart type is best for showing how a whole is divided into parts (e.g., budget by category)?
options: Line chart
Scatter plot
Pie chart
Bar chart
questions.answer: Pie chart
questions.explanation: Pie charts display parts of a whole as slices, making them ideal for showing proportional distributions like budget allocations.

questions.question: What does the '6 × 6 rule' for presentations suggest?
options: Use at least 6 fonts per slide
Use no more than 6 bullet points per slide and 6 words per bullet
Include 6 images and 6 charts
Have at least 6 slides with 6 paragraphs each
questions.answer: Use no more than 6 bullet points per slide and 6 words per bullet
questions.explanation: The 6×6 rule is a design guideline to keep slides uncluttered: max 6 bullet points per slide and 6 words per bullet.

questions.question: Real-time collaboration in Google Drive means:
options: Only one person can edit the document at a time
Multiple users can edit the same document simultaneously and see each other's changes live
The document is updated only when it is re-uploaded
Changes are saved only when the document is closed
questions.answer: Multiple users can edit the same document simultaneously and see each other's changes live
questions.explanation: Google Workspace tools support real-time collaboration, allowing multiple contributors to work simultaneously with live updates visible to all editors.

questions.question: The SIFT method for evaluating online information stands for:
options: Search, Identify, Find, Test
Stop, Investigate the source, Find better coverage, Trace claims
Scan, Interpret, Filter, Tag
Source, Skim, Infer, Transfer
questions.answer: Stop, Investigate the source, Find better coverage, Trace claims
questions.explanation: SIFT is a media literacy framework: Stop before sharing, Investigate the source, Find other coverage, and Trace claims to their origin.

questions.question: Which of the following best protects an account in addition to a strong password?
options: Sharing the password with a trusted friend
Using the same password across all accounts
Enabling two-factor authentication (2FA)
Writing the password on a sticky note
questions.answer: Enabling two-factor authentication (2FA)
questions.explanation: Two-factor authentication adds a second verification step (e.g., a code sent to your phone), making it significantly harder for attackers to access your account even if they know your password.

questions.question: A professional LinkedIn headline should:
options: Be your full legal name only
List every job you have ever had
Briefly describe your professional identity and value
Include your phone number and home address
questions.answer: Briefly describe your professional identity and value
questions.explanation: A LinkedIn headline is a short pitch that appears under your name. It should communicate who you are professionally and what you offer.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of descriptive analytics?
options: Predicting next month's sales using a model
Calculating the average customer age from a dataset
Recommending products based on past behavior
Automating a billing process
questions.answer: Calculating the average customer age from a dataset
questions.explanation: Descriptive analytics summarizes historical data (e.g., averages, totals, distributions) to describe what has happened.

questions.question: In project management, a Kanban board organizes tasks into columns such as:
options: Start, Middle, End
To Do, In Progress, Done
High, Medium, Low
Planning, Execution, Review, Close
questions.answer: To Do, In Progress, Done
questions.explanation: Kanban boards visualize workflow with columns representing stages of progress, most commonly To Do, In Progress, and Done.

questions.question: Which e-commerce business model charges customers a recurring fee for ongoing access to a product or service?
options: Marketplace model
Direct-to-consumer model
Subscription model
Auction model
questions.answer: Subscription model
questions.explanation: Subscription models (e.g., Netflix, Spotify) generate recurring revenue by charging customers a regular fee — monthly or annually — for continued access.

questions.question: Which of the following tasks is most likely to be automated by AI in the near future?
options: Writing original creative novels
Sorting and categorizing large volumes of data
Developing empathy and emotional intelligence
Performing complex ethical negotiations
questions.answer: Sorting and categorizing large volumes of data
questions.explanation: Repetitive, rule-based tasks like data sorting are well-suited for automation. Creative, emotional, and ethical tasks remain difficult for AI to replicate.

questions.question: Which formula would count only the cells in B1:B20 that contain numerical values (not blank or text)?
options: =SUM(B1:B20)
=COUNTA(B1:B20)
=COUNT(B1:B20)
=AVERAGE(B1:B20)
questions.answer: =COUNT(B1:B20)
questions.explanation: =COUNT counts only cells containing numbers. =COUNTA counts all non-empty cells including text.

questions.question: A pivot table in a spreadsheet is used to:
options: Automatically delete duplicate rows
Rotate a chart from horizontal to vertical
Summarize, group, and analyze large datasets interactively
Convert spreadsheet data to a PDF
questions.answer: Summarize, group, and analyze large datasets interactively
questions.explanation: Pivot tables let users quickly summarize and cross-tabulate large data sets by dragging fields into rows, columns, and values areas.

questions.question: The gig economy refers to:
options: Full-time employment at large corporations
A market of short-term, freelance, or contract-based work enabled by digital platforms
Government regulation of the tech industry
Physical concerts and entertainment events
questions.answer: A market of short-term, freelance, or contract-based work enabled by digital platforms
questions.explanation: The gig economy encompasses platforms like Uber, Fiverr, and Upwork, where workers take on flexible, project-based work rather than traditional employment.

questions.question: You are asked to analyze sales data for a small business. Describe the steps you would take in a spreadsheet to (1) calculate total and average monthly sales, (2) identify the top 3 months by revenue, and (3) create a visual to present your findings.
questions.answer: (1) Use =SUM() for total sales and =AVERAGE() for average monthly sales. (2) Sort the revenue column in descending order or use LARGE() to find the top 3 values. (3) Select the monthly data and insert a bar or column chart, add a title and axis labels, to clearly visualize revenue trends.
questions.explanation: Effective data analysis in a spreadsheet combines formulas for calculation, sorting/filtering for ranking, and charts for visual communication.

questions.question: Describe two ways that AI is currently changing the workplace and explain one skill that you believe will remain important for humans even as AI advances. Justify your answer.
questions.answer: AI changes: (1) automating repetitive tasks such as data entry, scheduling, and customer service chatbots, freeing workers for higher-level work; (2) enabling data-driven decision-making by quickly analyzing large datasets for insights. Enduring human skill: critical thinking / creativity — AI can process information but struggles to evaluate ethical implications, generate truly original ideas, or navigate complex human relationships. These require human judgment and context.
questions.explanation: While AI excels at pattern recognition and automation, skills like creativity, ethical reasoning, and interpersonal communication remain uniquely human strengths.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of software?
options: Printer
RAM chip
Google Chrome browser
Computer monitor
quizQuestions.answer: Google Chrome browser
quizQuestions.explanation: Software is a set of programs or instructions. Google Chrome is an application (software); the others are hardware.

quizQuestions.question: What does RAM stand for?
options: Remote Access Memory
Read-Only Access Module
Random Access Memory
Rapid Application Mode
quizQuestions.answer: Random Access Memory
quizQuestions.explanation: RAM (Random Access Memory) is the computer's short-term memory that holds data currently in use by the processor.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an operating system?
options: Microsoft Excel
macOS
Google Chrome
Adobe Photoshop
quizQuestions.answer: macOS
quizQuestions.explanation: macOS is Apple's operating system. Excel, Chrome, and Photoshop are applications that run on top of an operating system.

quizQuestions.question: File management best practice includes:
options: Saving all files to the desktop
Using descriptive file names and organized folder structures
Never renaming files once saved
Keeping all files in a single folder
quizQuestions.answer: Using descriptive file names and organized folder structures
quizQuestions.explanation: Good file management makes it easy to find, share, and back up files by using clear names and logical folder hierarchies.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an input device?
options: Monitor
Speaker
Mouse
Printer
quizQuestions.answer: Mouse
quizQuestions.explanation: Input devices send data to the computer. A mouse is an input device; monitors, speakers, and printers are output devices.

quizQuestions.question: Which greeting is most appropriate for a professional email to a potential employer?
options: Hey!
Yo boss,
Dear Mr. Kim,
What's up,
quizQuestions.answer: Dear Mr. Kim,
quizQuestions.explanation: Professional emails use formal greetings. 'Dear [Title Last Name],' is the standard respectful opening.

quizQuestions.question: What is the purpose of the CC field in an email?
options: To send the email anonymously
To send a copy to additional recipients who should be aware of the message
To attach files to the email
To encrypt the email
quizQuestions.answer: To send a copy to additional recipients who should be aware of the message
quizQuestions.explanation: CC (Carbon Copy) sends a visible copy to other recipients, signaling they are included for awareness but are not the primary audience.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is the best subject line for a professional email?
options: Hi
Question
Meeting Request — Project Kickoff — April 30
READ THIS IMMEDIATELY
quizQuestions.answer: Meeting Request — Project Kickoff — April 30
quizQuestions.explanation: A good subject line is specific and informative, telling the recipient exactly what the email is about at a glance.

quizQuestions.question: When should you use 'Reply All' in an email?
options: Always, to keep everyone informed
Only when your reply is relevant to everyone on the original email
Never — always reply only to the sender
When you want to disagree with the sender publicly
quizQuestions.answer: Only when your reply is relevant to everyone on the original email
quizQuestions.explanation: Reply All should be used thoughtfully — only when all recipients need to see your response. Overusing it clutters inboxes.

quizQuestions.question: Which closing is most appropriate for a formal professional email?
options: Later!
XOXO
Best regards,
Cya
quizQuestions.answer: Best regards,
quizQuestions.explanation: Professional email closings include 'Best regards,' 'Sincerely,' or 'Thank you,' followed by your full name.

quizQuestions.question: What is the purpose of using heading styles (Heading 1, Heading 2) in a document?
options: To change the document's page orientation
To create consistent formatting and enable a navigable outline
To add page numbers automatically
To insert a table of images
quizQuestions.answer: To create consistent formatting and enable a navigable outline
quizQuestions.explanation: Heading styles apply consistent formatting and allow document navigation tools and automatic table of contents generation.

quizQuestions.question: Which keyboard shortcut is used to make selected text bold in most word processors?
options: Ctrl + I
Ctrl + U
Ctrl + B
Ctrl + S
quizQuestions.answer: Ctrl + B
quizQuestions.explanation: Ctrl + B (Cmd + B on Mac) applies bold formatting to selected text in Google Docs, Microsoft Word, and most word processors.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'margin' refer to in document design?
options: The font size used in the document
The blank space around the edges of a page
The number of words per line
The color of the page background
quizQuestions.answer: The blank space around the edges of a page
quizQuestions.explanation: Margins are the white space borders on all four sides of a page that frame the content and improve readability.

quizQuestions.question: What is a document template?
options: A blank page with no formatting
A pre-designed document with layouts, fonts, and styles ready to use
A saved version of a deleted file
A type of spreadsheet
quizQuestions.answer: A pre-designed document with layouts, fonts, and styles ready to use
quizQuestions.explanation: Templates provide a starting point with pre-set formatting, saving time and ensuring consistent professional appearance.

quizQuestions.question: Which line spacing setting provides the most white space between lines, improving readability for longer documents?
options: Single (1.0)
Double (2.0)
Condensed (0.8)
quizQuestions.answer: Double (2.0)
quizQuestions.explanation: Double spacing creates the most space between lines, making text easier to read and allowing room for annotations — common in academic documents.

quizQuestions.question: In a spreadsheet, a cell reference like 'B3' refers to:
options: Row B, Column 3
Column B, Row 3
The 3rd sheet, column B
The 2nd block of data
quizQuestions.answer: Column B, Row 3
quizQuestions.explanation: Cell references use the column letter followed by the row number. B3 is the cell at Column B, Row 3.

quizQuestions.question: What does the =COUNT(A1:A10) formula do?
options: Adds all values in A1 to A10
Returns the average of A1:A10
Counts the number of cells in A1:A10 that contain numbers
Finds the largest value in A1:A10
quizQuestions.answer: Counts the number of cells in A1:A10 that contain numbers
quizQuestions.explanation: =COUNT counts only cells with numeric data in the specified range.

quizQuestions.question: What is an absolute cell reference in a spreadsheet (e.g., $A$1)?
options: A cell reference that changes when you copy a formula to another cell
A cell reference that stays fixed regardless of where the formula is copied
A reference to a cell in another spreadsheet file
A cell that cannot be edited
quizQuestions.answer: A cell reference that stays fixed regardless of where the formula is copied
quizQuestions.explanation: Adding $ signs locks the column and/or row reference. $A$1 always points to cell A1 no matter where the formula is moved.

quizQuestions.question: The formula =IF(B2>100, 'Over Budget', 'OK') will display 'Over Budget' when:
options: B2 equals exactly 100
B2 is less than 100
B2 is greater than 100
B2 is empty
quizQuestions.answer: B2 is greater than 100
quizQuestions.explanation: The IF function checks the logical condition (B2>100). If true, it returns 'Over Budget'; otherwise it returns 'OK'.

quizQuestions.question: Which formula finds the average of cells C1 through C8?
options: =MEAN(C1:C8)
=AVG(C1:C8)
=AVERAGE(C1:C8)
=SUM(C1:C8)/COUNT(C1:C8) only
quizQuestions.answer: =AVERAGE(C1:C8)
quizQuestions.explanation: =AVERAGE(range) is the built-in function that calculates the arithmetic mean. While SUM/COUNT also works, =AVERAGE is the standard formula.

quizQuestions.question: What does sorting data in a spreadsheet allow you to do?
options: Delete duplicate rows automatically
Arrange data in ascending or descending order based on a column
Change the font of all cells
Merge multiple sheets into one
quizQuestions.answer: Arrange data in ascending or descending order based on a column
quizQuestions.explanation: Sorting reorders rows based on values in a selected column, either from A to Z (ascending) or Z to A (descending).

quizQuestions.question: What does a filter in a spreadsheet do?
options: Changes the color of cells based on value
Hides rows that do not meet specified criteria, making it easier to focus on relevant data
Removes all formulas from a range
Copies data to a new sheet
quizQuestions.answer: Hides rows that do not meet specified criteria, making it easier to focus on relevant data
quizQuestions.explanation: Filters temporarily hide rows that don't match your criteria, allowing you to view and analyze a subset of your data.

quizQuestions.question: In VLOOKUP, the third argument (col_index_num) specifies:
options: The column to search in
The value to look up
Which column number to return a value from
Whether the match is exact or approximate
quizQuestions.answer: Which column number to return a value from
quizQuestions.explanation: The third argument in VLOOKUP tells the function which column (counting from the left of the lookup range) to retrieve the result from.

quizQuestions.question: A pivot table is most useful for:
options: Writing macros to automate tasks
Summarizing and cross-tabulating large datasets quickly
Creating animated charts
Formatting cells with conditional rules
quizQuestions.answer: Summarizing and cross-tabulating large datasets quickly
quizQuestions.explanation: Pivot tables let you group, count, sum, and average large amounts of data interactively without writing formulas.

quizQuestions.question: Which chart type is best for showing a trend over time (e.g., monthly sales for a year)?
options: Pie chart
Bar chart
Line chart
Scatter plot
quizQuestions.answer: Line chart
quizQuestions.explanation: Line charts connect data points over time, making trends, growth, and changes easy to see across a continuous period.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a best practice for slide text?
options: Include complete paragraphs so the audience can read everything
Use bullet points with brief phrases, not full sentences
Use 3 different fonts per slide for variety
Avoid using any text and rely only on images
quizQuestions.answer: Use bullet points with brief phrases, not full sentences
quizQuestions.explanation: Slides should support the speaker, not replace them. Brief bullet points keep slides clean and the audience focused on the presenter.

quizQuestions.question: A slide's color palette should ideally consist of:
options: As many colors as possible to look creative
2–3 complementary colors used consistently
Only black and white
A different color for every slide
quizQuestions.answer: 2–3 complementary colors used consistently
quizQuestions.explanation: A limited, consistent color palette creates visual cohesion and professionalism. Too many colors look chaotic and distract from the content.

quizQuestions.question: What is the purpose of a title slide in a presentation?
options: To list every topic that will be covered in detail
To introduce the topic, presenter, and context to the audience
To display the data and conclusions
To show the references and sources used
quizQuestions.answer: To introduce the topic, presenter, and context to the audience
quizQuestions.explanation: The title slide sets the stage — it shows the presentation topic, the presenter's name, and often the date or organization.

quizQuestions.question: Visual hierarchy in slide design means:
options: Using the same font size for all text on every slide
Arranging elements so the most important information is visually dominant
Adding animations to every element
Placing images only at the bottom of each slide
quizQuestions.answer: Arranging elements so the most important information is visually dominant
quizQuestions.explanation: Visual hierarchy guides the audience's eye, ensuring key messages are noticed first through size, contrast, color, or position.

quizQuestions.question: What is 'white space' in slide design?
options: The background color of the slide
Empty space around elements that improves readability and focus
Slides with no content as placeholders
A font style used in presentations
quizQuestions.answer: Empty space around elements that improves readability and focus
quizQuestions.explanation: White space (or negative space) is intentional empty space around elements. It prevents clutter and makes content easier to read and process.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a benefit of using cloud storage for team projects?
options: Only one person can work on a file at a time
Files can be accessed and edited from any device with internet access
Files are permanently deleted after 30 days
Cloud storage does not require an internet connection
quizQuestions.answer: Files can be accessed and edited from any device with internet access
quizQuestions.explanation: Cloud storage enables access from any internet-connected device, supporting remote collaboration and eliminating the need to email files back and forth.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'version history' allow you to do in Google Docs?
options: Delete all previous versions of a document
See who made changes and restore earlier versions of the document
Share the document with new users
Convert the document to a spreadsheet
quizQuestions.answer: See who made changes and restore earlier versions of the document
quizQuestions.explanation: Version history tracks every saved change, letting collaborators see who edited what and when, and restore earlier versions if needed.

quizQuestions.question: When sharing a file via Google Drive, 'Editor' access allows the recipient to:
options: View only
Comment only
View, comment, and make changes to the file
View and print but not download
quizQuestions.answer: View, comment, and make changes to the file
quizQuestions.explanation: Editor access grants full editing rights — the recipient can add, modify, and delete content in the shared file.

quizQuestions.question: SaaS stands for:
options: Software as a Standard
Security and Application Service
Software as a Service
Systems and Administration Setup
quizQuestions.answer: Software as a Service
quizQuestions.explanation: SaaS delivers software applications over the internet on a subscription basis (e.g., Google Workspace, Microsoft 365), eliminating local installation.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of cloud collaboration?
options: Emailing a Word document attachment back and forth
Two students editing the same Google Doc simultaneously from different locations
Saving a file to a USB drive and passing it between classmates
Printing a document and writing comments by hand
quizQuestions.answer: Two students editing the same Google Doc simultaneously from different locations
quizQuestions.explanation: Real-time cloud collaboration means multiple users can work on the same live document at the same time from any location.

quizQuestions.question: Which Boolean search operator would you use to exclude a word from search results?
options: AND
OR
NOT
WITH
quizQuestions.answer: NOT
quizQuestions.explanation: The Boolean operator NOT (or a minus sign in Google) excludes terms from results. E.g., 'jaguar NOT car' returns results about the animal, not the vehicle.

quizQuestions.question: Which domain extension is most commonly associated with government websites in the United States?
options: .com
.org
.edu
.gov
quizQuestions.answer: .gov
quizQuestions.explanation: The .gov domain is restricted to U.S. government entities, making it a reliable indicator of official government information.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is the best indicator that a website is a reliable source?
options: It has many colorful images
It cites peer-reviewed studies and identifies its authors
It appears at the top of Google search results
It has been shared many times on social media
quizQuestions.answer: It cites peer-reviewed studies and identifies its authors
quizQuestions.explanation: Credible sources identify their authors, cite verifiable evidence, and have editorial oversight — not simply high traffic or eye-catching design.

quizQuestions.question: What does it mean to 'trace a claim to its origin'?
options: Find the original source of the information rather than relying on a secondary report
Copy the article and paste it into a new document
Search for the newest article about the topic
Find the most popular article about the topic
quizQuestions.answer: Find the original source of the information rather than relying on a secondary report
quizQuestions.explanation: Tracing claims means following citations or links back to the primary source to verify what the original research or data actually says.

quizQuestions.question: Putting a phrase in quotation marks in a Google search:
options: Translates the phrase to another language
Searches for pages with that exact phrase
Excludes pages containing that phrase
Sorts results by date published
quizQuestions.answer: Searches for pages with that exact phrase
quizQuestions.explanation: Enclosing a phrase in quotes tells the search engine to return only results that contain that exact sequence of words.

quizQuestions.question: What is malware?
options: A type of productivity software
Software designed to damage, disrupt, or gain unauthorized access to computer systems
A cloud storage service
A programming language
quizQuestions.answer: Software designed to damage, disrupt, or gain unauthorized access to computer systems
quizQuestions.explanation: Malware (malicious software) includes viruses, ransomware, spyware, and trojans — all designed to harm or exploit systems and data.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is the best practice for creating a secure password?
options: Use your birthdate for easy remembering
Use a combination of uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols with 12+ characters
Use the same password for all accounts to avoid forgetting
Use a common dictionary word
quizQuestions.answer: Use a combination of uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols with 12+ characters
quizQuestions.explanation: Long passwords with mixed character types are exponentially harder to crack with brute-force attacks than short or simple passwords.

quizQuestions.question: Phishing attacks typically try to:
options: Improve your computer's performance
Trick you into revealing sensitive information like passwords or credit card numbers
Update your software automatically
Block unwanted advertisements
quizQuestions.answer: Trick you into revealing sensitive information like passwords or credit card numbers
quizQuestions.explanation: Phishing uses deceptive emails, texts, or websites that impersonate trusted organizations to steal credentials or personal information.

quizQuestions.question: Two-factor authentication (2FA) improves account security by:
options: Requiring two different passwords
Adding a second verification step beyond just a password
Logging you out automatically after 2 minutes
Blocking access from unrecognized email addresses
quizQuestions.answer: Adding a second verification step beyond just a password
quizQuestions.explanation: 2FA requires something you know (password) plus something you have (e.g., a code sent to your phone), making unauthorized access much harder.

quizQuestions.question: Which is an example of safe browsing practice?
options: Clicking all pop-up ads that offer free prizes
Downloading software from unknown third-party websites
Checking for HTTPS in the URL before entering personal information
Using public Wi-Fi to access your bank account without a VPN
quizQuestions.answer: Checking for HTTPS in the URL before entering personal information
quizQuestions.explanation: HTTPS indicates an encrypted connection. Always verify HTTPS before entering passwords, payment details, or other sensitive information.

quizQuestions.question: Your digital footprint includes:
options: Only the photos you post publicly
Only emails you send
All data traces from your online activities including searches, posts, purchases, and logins
Only information on government databases
quizQuestions.answer: All data traces from your online activities including searches, posts, purchases, and logins
quizQuestions.explanation: Your digital footprint is the cumulative record of everything you do online — it is far broader than just public posts.

quizQuestions.question: On LinkedIn, your 'headline' appears:
options: Only on your private settings page
Directly below your name and is one of the first things people see on your profile
Only when you apply for a job
At the bottom of your profile under recommendations
quizQuestions.answer: Directly below your name and is one of the first things people see on your profile
quizQuestions.explanation: The LinkedIn headline is a prominent, brief description of who you are professionally — it's visible in search results and connection requests.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following would be most harmful to a professional reputation online?
options: Posting photos from a school science fair
Sharing an article about industry trends
Posting offensive comments on public social media
Updating your LinkedIn profile with new skills
quizQuestions.answer: Posting offensive comments on public social media
quizQuestions.explanation: Employers regularly search candidates online. Offensive or inappropriate public content can damage professional relationships and job prospects.

quizQuestions.question: The purpose of a LinkedIn 'About' section is to:
options: List every job title you have ever had in detail
Provide a brief personal narrative summarizing your skills, interests, and professional goals
Display your GPA and test scores
Show a gallery of your personal photos
quizQuestions.answer: Provide a brief personal narrative summarizing your skills, interests, and professional goals
quizQuestions.explanation: The About section is your elevator pitch — a short, engaging summary that tells your professional story and what you bring to the table.

quizQuestions.question: Which behavior helps maintain a positive professional digital brand?
options: Posting controversial political opinions under your real name frequently
Engaging professionally with industry content and connecting with mentors on LinkedIn
Keeping your LinkedIn profile empty to protect privacy
Using the same username for gaming and professional networking
quizQuestions.answer: Engaging professionally with industry content and connecting with mentors on LinkedIn
quizQuestions.explanation: Actively engaging with relevant content and building a professional network demonstrates initiative and enhances your credibility in your field.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of quantitative data?
options: A customer's favorite color
The number of units sold per month
A product review written in words
A country's name
quizQuestions.answer: The number of units sold per month
quizQuestions.explanation: Quantitative data is numerical and measurable. Sales counts are numbers; the other examples are categorical or qualitative.

quizQuestions.question: The median of a dataset is the:
options: Most frequently occurring value
Sum of all values divided by the count
Middle value when data is sorted in order
Difference between the largest and smallest values
quizQuestions.answer: Middle value when data is sorted in order
quizQuestions.explanation: The median is the middle value in an ordered dataset. It is resistant to extreme outliers, unlike the mean.

quizQuestions.question: A business dashboard typically displays:
options: The source code of the company's website
Key performance indicators and metrics in visual format for quick decision-making
Employee personal information
Raw spreadsheet data in text form only
quizQuestions.answer: Key performance indicators and metrics in visual format for quick decision-making
quizQuestions.explanation: Dashboards aggregate key metrics (KPIs) into charts and summaries, giving managers a quick overview of business performance.

quizQuestions.question: Descriptive analytics answers the question:
options: What will happen next?
Why did this happen?
What happened?
How can we make it happen?
quizQuestions.answer: What happened?
quizQuestions.explanation: Descriptive analytics summarizes historical data to describe past events. Predictive analytics forecasts what will happen; prescriptive analytics recommends actions.

quizQuestions.question: If a dataset has values 4, 7, 7, 9, and 13, what is the mode?
quizQuestions.explanation: The mode is the value that appears most frequently. '7' appears twice in the dataset, more than any other value.

quizQuestions.question: In project management, a 'deadline' refers to:
options: The budget limit for a project
The date by which a task or project must be completed
The team member responsible for a task
The list of project goals
quizQuestions.answer: The date by which a task or project must be completed
quizQuestions.explanation: A deadline is the final date or time by which work must be finished. Meeting deadlines is a core professional responsibility.

quizQuestions.question: Which tool would you use to visually manage tasks in a Kanban-style workflow?
options: Google Sheets
Trello
Gmail
Google Slides
quizQuestions.answer: Trello
quizQuestions.explanation: Trello uses a Kanban board interface with cards and columns (e.g., To Do, In Progress, Done) to visually manage tasks and workflows.

quizQuestions.question: Breaking a large project into smaller tasks is useful because:
options: It makes the project look more complicated
It allows progress to be tracked and makes work less overwhelming
It removes the need for deadlines
It means only one person needs to work on the project
quizQuestions.answer: It allows progress to be tracked and makes work less overwhelming
quizQuestions.explanation: Decomposing projects into subtasks makes them manageable, enables progress tracking, and allows work to be distributed across team members.

quizQuestions.question: Assigning an 'owner' to a task in a project management tool means:
options: That person paid for the task
That person is responsible for completing that specific task
That person cannot be changed or reassigned
The company owns the task outcome
quizQuestions.answer: That person is responsible for completing that specific task
quizQuestions.explanation: Assigning ownership clarifies accountability — each task has one person clearly responsible for its completion by the deadline.

quizQuestions.question: A project status update to a manager should include:
options: Only information about what went wrong
Progress made, upcoming tasks, and any blockers or risks
Only the final results once the project is complete
Personal opinions about team members
quizQuestions.answer: Progress made, upcoming tasks, and any blockers or risks
quizQuestions.explanation: Effective status updates communicate accomplishments, next steps, and any issues that need attention — keeping stakeholders informed without overwhelming them.

quizQuestions.question: An online marketplace like Amazon allows:
options: Only one seller to list products
Multiple third-party sellers to list and sell products to consumers on a single platform
Buyers to set their own prices
Only physical goods to be sold
quizQuestions.answer: Multiple third-party sellers to list and sell products to consumers on a single platform
quizQuestions.explanation: Marketplaces aggregate sellers and buyers on one platform, earning revenue through listing fees or commissions on sales.

quizQuestions.question: A subscription business model generates revenue by:
options: Charging a one-time fee per purchase
Selling physical products in a store
Charging recurring fees (monthly or annual) for continued access to a product or service
Auctioning products to the highest bidder
quizQuestions.answer: Charging recurring fees (monthly or annual) for continued access to a product or service
quizQuestions.explanation: Subscription models (Netflix, Spotify, Adobe) provide predictable recurring revenue and build long-term customer relationships.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of a digital payment method?
options: Writing a personal check
Paying with cash at a store
Using PayPal to purchase online
Using a money order
quizQuestions.answer: Using PayPal to purchase online
quizQuestions.explanation: PayPal is a digital payment platform that processes electronic transactions online without requiring physical cash or checks.

quizQuestions.question: Direct-to-consumer (DTC) e-commerce means a brand:
options: Sells products through third-party retailers only
Sells directly to customers via its own website without intermediaries
Only sells to other businesses (B2B)
Only sells at physical stores
quizQuestions.answer: Sells directly to customers via its own website without intermediaries
quizQuestions.explanation: DTC brands (e.g., Warby Parker, Glossier) bypass traditional retail middlemen to sell directly to consumers, retaining more margin and control of customer experience.

quizQuestions.question: Customer experience (CX) in e-commerce refers to:
options: The technical specifications of the website's servers
The overall impression a customer has across all interactions with a brand online
The number of products listed on the website
The discount percentage offered on sale items
quizQuestions.answer: The overall impression a customer has across all interactions with a brand online
quizQuestions.explanation: Customer experience encompasses everything from website usability and checkout ease to delivery speed, returns policy, and customer support.

quizQuestions.question: Artificial intelligence (AI) refers to:
options: Software that only follows pre-programmed rules with no flexibility
Computer systems that can perform tasks that typically require human intelligence, such as learning and problem-solving
Hardware that runs faster than standard computers
A type of cybersecurity software
quizQuestions.answer: Computer systems that can perform tasks that typically require human intelligence, such as learning and problem-solving
quizQuestions.explanation: AI encompasses machine learning, natural language processing, and computer vision — technologies that enable computers to learn from data and make decisions.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following jobs is most at risk of automation?
options: Therapist
Data entry clerk
Elementary school teacher
Art director
quizQuestions.answer: Data entry clerk
quizQuestions.explanation: Repetitive, rule-based tasks like data entry are highly susceptible to automation. Roles requiring human judgment, creativity, and emotional intelligence are harder to automate.

quizQuestions.question: The gig economy is best characterized by:
options: Permanent, full-time employment with benefits
Short-term, flexible, freelance or contract work arranged through digital platforms
Government-funded employment programs
Traditional 9-to-5 office jobs
quizQuestions.answer: Short-term, flexible, freelance or contract work arranged through digital platforms
quizQuestions.explanation: Gig economy workers (e.g., Uber drivers, Fiverr freelancers) take on project-based work with flexibility but without traditional employment benefits.

quizQuestions.question: Which skill is considered most 'future-proof' in a world increasingly shaped by AI and automation?
options: Memorizing large amounts of factual data
Performing fast manual data calculations
Critical thinking and creative problem-solving
Operating a single specialized machine
quizQuestions.answer: Critical thinking and creative problem-solving
quizQuestions.explanation: While AI can process and recall data far faster than humans, it struggles with nuanced reasoning, creativity, and applying judgment in novel situations — making these skills uniquely valuable.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'digital literacy' mean in the context of career readiness?
options: Being able to read e-books on a tablet
Having the ability to use digital tools effectively, evaluate online information critically, and adapt to new technologies
Typing at 100 words per minute
Having a social media account
quizQuestions.answer: Having the ability to use digital tools effectively, evaluate online information critically, and adapt to new technologies
quizQuestions.explanation: Digital literacy goes beyond basic computer use — it includes critical thinking about technology, safe online behavior, and the adaptability to learn new tools as they emerge.
